title: Overview
text: Anvil is a log-structured merge-tree storage engine designed for workloads dominated by sequential writes rather than random updates. Incoming writes land first in an in-memory structure called the memtable, which holds recent writes sorted by key before they are ever written to disk. Once the memtable reaches a configured size threshold, Anvil flushes it to disk as an immutable sorted file, sometimes called a sorted string table or SSTable. Because SSTables are immutable once written, Anvil never modifies a file in place; instead, updates and deletes are represented as new entries that shadow older ones with the same key. A read for a given key must therefore check the memtable first, then progressively older SSTables, until either a matching entry or a tombstone marking a deletion is found. Without further intervention this read path would grow steadily slower as more SSTables accumulate, which is precisely the problem compaction exists to solve.

title: Memtable
text: The memtable is typically implemented as a skip list or a balanced tree, chosen specifically for efficient ordered insertion and in-order iteration during flush. Anvil additionally writes every incoming operation to a write-ahead log before acknowledging it, so an in-memory-only memtable can be reconstructed after a crash. The write-ahead log is append-only and is truncated only after the corresponding memtable has been safely flushed to an SSTable on disk. Memtable size is bounded primarily by available memory and by how long the engine is willing to delay a flush, since a larger memtable means fewer, larger SSTables. Anvil's default configuration flushes a memtable once it reaches 64 megabytes, a value chosen to balance flush frequency against per-flush overhead.

title: Compaction strategies
text: Compaction merges multiple SSTables into fewer, larger ones, discarding shadowed entries and tombstones whose deletion has propagated to every older file. Anvil supports two compaction strategies: a tiered strategy that merges same-size files together, and a leveled strategy that organizes SSTables into size-based levels with bounded overlap. Tiered compaction favors write throughput, since it merges less often, but tolerates more read amplification because a key may exist unmerged across several same-tier files simultaneously. Leveled compaction favors read performance, since each level guarantees non-overlapping key ranges, but pays for this with substantially higher write amplification from more frequent merging. Anvil defaults to leveled compaction for general-purpose deployments and recommends tiered compaction specifically for write-heavy, append-only workloads like log ingestion.

title: Bloom filters
text: Every SSTable carries an accompanying Bloom filter summarizing which keys the file might contain, letting a read skip files that provably do not hold the requested key. A Bloom filter can produce false positives, incorrectly suggesting a key might be present, but never false negatives, so a read never skips a file that actually contains its target key. Anvil sizes each Bloom filter to target a false-positive rate of roughly one percent by default, trading additional memory for fewer wasted disk reads. Lowering the target false-positive rate further reduces wasted reads but increases the Bloom filter's memory footprint per SSTable, a tradeoff operators can tune per workload. Without Bloom filters, a read for a missing key would need to check every SSTable on disk, since the absence of a key cannot otherwise be ruled out cheaply.

title: Write amplification
text: Write amplification measures how many bytes are actually written to disk for each byte of application data, and it is one of the central tradeoffs in any LSM design. Every compaction pass rewrites existing data to merge it with newer entries, so a single logical write can be physically rewritten several times over its lifetime before it is finally discarded or superseded. Leveled compaction typically produces higher write amplification than tiered compaction, since data written to one level is eventually rewritten again when merged into the next. Anvil's leveled compaction under default settings produces a measured write amplification factor of roughly ten, meaning each byte of application data results in about ten bytes of total disk writes over its lifetime. Solid-state storage's limited write endurance makes write amplification a genuine operational concern, not merely a throughput consideration, since excessive rewriting shortens the underlying storage hardware's usable lifetime.

title: Read path
text: A point read in Anvil checks the memtable first, then each relevant SSTable from newest to oldest, stopping as soon as a matching key or tombstone is found. Bloom filters let this check skip most SSTables cheaply, so in practice a typical point read touches only a small handful of files rather than the entire set. Range reads are more expensive, since they cannot rely on Bloom filters at all and must merge overlapping key ranges from every relevant SSTable using a heap-based merge iterator. Leveled compaction's non-overlapping key ranges within a level substantially reduce the number of files a range read must merge, compared to tiered compaction's looser overlap guarantees. Anvil caches recently accessed SSTable index blocks in memory, which measurably reduces the disk I/O cost of repeated reads over a similar key range.

title: Tuning guidance
text: Operators tuning Anvil for a specific workload generally start by choosing a compaction strategy matching their read-versus-write ratio, before adjusting any lower-level parameter. Memtable size is the next parameter operators typically adjust, since a larger memtable produces fewer, larger SSTables and reduces compaction frequency at the cost of higher memory use and longer crash-recovery replay. Bloom filter false-positive rate is usually the last parameter tuned, since its effect is smaller than compaction strategy or memtable size for most workloads. Anvil ships with three named presets, write-heavy, balanced, and read-heavy, that bundle sensible defaults for all three parameters together rather than requiring operators to tune each independently. Most production deployments start from one of these presets and adjust only a single parameter afterward, rather than tuning from Anvil's raw defaults directly.

title: Version history
text: Anvil 1.0 released in March 2019 supported only tiered compaction, with leveled compaction added later as an alternative strategy. Anvil 1.4, released in January 2020, added leveled compaction as a selectable strategy alongside the original tiered approach. Anvil 2.0, shipped in August 2021, introduced configurable Bloom filter false-positive targets, replacing a previously fixed two-percent rate. Anvil 2.3, released in May 2022, added the three named tuning presets described in the tuning guidance section. A planned 2.5 release will extend configurable presets to cover the write-ahead log's own durability mode.

title: GPU-accelerated compaction dataset (ALCB anchor)
text: Several research groups have explored offloading compaction's merge-sort step to GPU hardware, evaluating results against the Aurora LSM Compaction Benchmark, known as ALCB. The ALCB reference dataset used in these studies totals 2.1 terabytes of synthetic key-value data, modeled on a mix of write-heavy and read-heavy access patterns. One published evaluation using Anvil's compaction engine against the ALCB dataset reported a 3.4x compaction throughput improvement when merge-sort was offloaded to GPU hardware. The same study noted that GPU offloading provided the largest benefit for leveled compaction specifically, since leveled compaction performs proportionally more merge-sort work per byte written.

title: Tessera GPU scheduler reference (Tessera anchor)
text: The Tessera GPU scheduler is a widely used research scheduler for coordinating many concurrent GPU kernels across shared hardware, and it was the scheduler used in the ALCB compaction offloading study. Tessera, as configured for that study, supported a maximum of 96 concurrent kernels per GPU device before additional kernels were queued rather than dispatched immediately. That concurrency ceiling meant the compaction study's throughput results reflect Tessera's specific scheduling limits rather than the theoretical maximum parallelism the underlying GPU hardware could otherwise support. Later compaction-offloading studies using newer scheduler versions have reported different concurrency ceilings, which complicates direct throughput comparisons across studies published in different years.

title: Compaction strategy comparison
text:
The tradeoffs between Anvil's two compaction strategies are summarized in the following comparison. | Strategy | Write amplification | Read amplification | Space amplification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tiered | 4x | 8x | 2.0x |
| Leveled | 10x | 2x | 1.1x | Space amplification measures how much disk space the engine uses beyond the logical size of the stored data, and leveled compaction's tighter bound comes directly from its more aggressive merging. Operators are encouraged to re-measure these ratios against their own workload rather than relying solely on this reference table.

title: Bloom filter sizing
text:
Bloom filter memory cost grows as the target false-positive rate shrinks, following the standard Bloom filter bits-per-key relationship. | Target false-positive rate | Bits per key | Memory per million keys |
|---|---|---|
| 5% | 4.6 | 575 KB |
| 1% | 9.6 | 1.2 MB |
| 0.1% | 14.4 | 1.8 MB | Anvil's one-percent default sits near the point of diminishing returns, where further reductions in false-positive rate cost meaningfully more memory per additional percentage point avoided.

title: Compaction troubleshooting checklist
text:
Operators seeing degraded read latency should work through the following checklist before escalating to a full reconfiguration. 1. Check whether compaction is falling behind the incoming write rate, visible as a steadily growing SSTable count.
2. Confirm Bloom filters are enabled and sized appropriately for the current key cardinality.
3. Verify available disk I/O bandwidth is not saturated by concurrent compaction and foreground read traffic.
4. Review whether the chosen compaction strategy still matches the workload's current read-write ratio.
5. Check for pathologically large individual values that might be inflating per-file compaction cost. The first item, compaction falling behind, is by far the most common root cause reported across Anvil deployments, according to the project's public incident archive.

title: Deployment checklist for new clusters
text:
New Anvil deployments should complete the following steps before being exposed to production traffic. 1. Choose an initial compaction strategy based on the expected read-write ratio.
2. Provision disk I/O bandwidth with headroom for background compaction alongside foreground traffic.
3. Configure write-ahead log replication or durable storage appropriate to the deployment's durability requirements.
4. Run the reference benchmark against representative sample data before accepting production traffic.
5. Set up monitoring for SSTable count and compaction lag as leading indicators of future degradation. Skipping the reference benchmark step is the most common cause of unpleasant surprises during a cluster's first real traffic spike.

title: International LSM research
text: Foundational LSM-tree research spans several language traditions beyond the English-language papers most engineers encounter first. Matsumoto and Endo's ログ構造化マージツリーにおける書き込み増幅の解析 (2015) provides one of the earliest detailed write-amplification analyses that later leveled-compaction designs, including Anvil's, cite directly. Volkova's Оптимизация компактации в LSM-деревьях (2018) proposes a hybrid compaction scheduling heuristic that influenced Anvil's own compaction scheduler. Anagnostou's study of συμπίεση δεδομένων σε δομές LSM (data compaction in LSM structures) situates both results within a broader survey still cited in later papers. Anvil's design documentation cites all three works directly in its compaction chapter.

title: Write amplification bound
text: Leveled compaction's write amplification can be bounded analytically in terms of the level size ratio, commonly denoted T. For a system with L levels and a size ratio T between adjacent levels, the standard bound on write amplification is approximately L * T, since each level's data is rewritten roughly T times before moving to the next level. With Anvil's default size ratio of T = 10 and a typical deployment reaching L = 4 levels before data reaches the largest tier, this bound predicts a write amplification of roughly 40 in the worst case. The measured value of roughly 10 reported in the write amplification section is well below this worst-case bound, reflecting that not every level reaches its maximum size simultaneously in a typical workload. Reducing T lowers the worst-case bound but increases the number of levels needed to hold the same total data volume, trading write amplification against metadata overhead.

title: History: from a single-strategy engine to configurable compaction
text: Anvil's earliest prototype, built in 2018, supported only a single hard-coded compaction strategy resembling what later became the tiered approach, with no leveled alternative and no configurable parameters at all. That rigidity was a deliberate simplification during the prototype phase, letting the team focus on getting the core memtable, write-ahead log, and SSTable format correct before adding strategy flexibility. The first production deployments, run internally against a write-heavy logging workload, performed well under this single strategy, and there was initially little pressure to add an alternative. Pressure changed once a second internal team adopted Anvil for a read-heavy lookup service, and quickly found tiered compaction's read amplification unacceptable for their latency requirements. That team's complaints directly motivated the leveled compaction implementation, added specifically to give read-heavy workloads a non-overlapping key range guarantee within each level. Implementing leveled compaction took considerably longer than the original tiered strategy, since it required a new level-management component tracking size thresholds and triggering merges across level boundaries automatically. An early leveled-compaction implementation had a subtle bug where a burst of writes could temporarily leave a level far above its target size, triggering a cascading series of merges that briefly starved foreground reads of I/O bandwidth. That bug was fixed by adding a rate limiter specifically for compaction I/O, capping how much bandwidth background merging could consume relative to foreground traffic during a compaction backlog. The rate limiter shipped in the 1.4 release alongside leveled compaction itself, since the team considered leveled compaction unsafe to ship without it after the cascading-merge incident during internal testing. Bloom filters existed from the very first prototype, but with a fixed two-percent false-positive rate that was never intended as a long-term default, simply the first reasonable value the original author picked. Making the false-positive rate configurable, in the 2.0 release, came after several teams independently asked for a way to trade memory for fewer wasted disk reads, each having discovered the fixed rate empirically through their own benchmarking. The three named tuning presets introduced in 2.3 were, in a sense, an admission that most operators did not want to tune three independent parameters separately, and preferred a small number of well-tested bundles instead. Internal usage data collected after the 2.3 release confirmed this: the large majority of new deployments now start from one of the three presets rather than Anvil's raw underlying defaults. Looking back, the team identifies the internal read-heavy team's original complaint as the single event that most shaped Anvil's subsequent design, since nearly every major feature added since, leveled compaction, the compaction rate limiter, and configurable Bloom filters, traces back to that same underlying tension between write and read performance.

title: Incident postmortem: a compaction backlog on an undersized cluster
text: In early 2022, an internal deployment running Anvil's write-heavy preset experienced a sustained compaction backlog after a sustained increase in write volume roughly tripled the cluster's typical ingestion rate. The write-heavy preset favors tiered compaction specifically to prioritize write throughput, but tiered compaction's higher read amplification meant that as unmerged files accumulated, read latency degraded far faster than the operations team had anticipated. SSTable count on the affected nodes grew from a typical steady-state count of around 40 files to over 300 files within six hours, each additional file adding a small but cumulative cost to every point read. Bloom filters kept most of that read-amplification cost from becoming even worse, since reads still skipped the large majority of the accumulated files, but the sheer file count eventually overwhelmed even that mitigation. The immediate operational response was to manually throttle incoming writes at the application layer, giving compaction a chance to catch up without the write rate continuing to outpace it. Once compaction caught up, SSTable count returned to its typical steady-state range within roughly four hours, and read latency recovered to its pre-incident baseline shortly afterward. The postmortem's primary finding was that the write-heavy preset's underlying assumption, that read latency matters less than write throughput, does not hold once SSTable count grows far enough, since eventually enough unmerged files degrade reads regardless of Bloom filter coverage. As a direct result, the team added a new monitoring alert specifically for SSTable count exceeding a fixed threshold, rather than relying solely on aggregate read-latency metrics that lagged the underlying cause by nearly an hour. A second recommendation, still under discussion, proposes an adaptive preset that automatically shifts compaction priority toward reads once SSTable count crosses a configurable threshold, rather than requiring an operator to intervene manually. The incident is now used as the reference case internally whenever a new deployment proposes using the write-heavy preset under a workload with any meaningful read traffic at all. No data was lost during the incident, since the write-ahead log continued accepting and durably recording writes throughout; the backlog affected only compaction and downstream read latency, never write durability. A capacity review completed the following quarter increased the affected cluster's provisioned disk I/O bandwidth by fifty percent, sized so that compaction can keep pace with triple the typical ingestion rate before any backlog begins to form. The same review standardized the SSTable-count alert threshold across every internal Anvil deployment, replacing the per-team values that had previously varied widely and had left some clusters alerting far later than others under identical conditions. Neither change required modifying the engine itself, and both are recorded in the deployment checklist's revision history.

title: Summary: memtable flush trigger
text: This recap restates the memtable flush trigger for readers skimming only the overview section. Once the memtable reaches a configured memory threshold, Anvil flushes it to disk as an immutable sorted file, sometimes called a sorted string table or SSTable. The exact threshold value is configurable per deployment and is discussed in full in the memtable section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the underlying flush behavior; it exists purely as a quick reference for onboarding new operators. Teams porting Anvil's design to another storage engine should read the full memtable section before reimplementing this behavior themselves. This summary is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.

title: Summary: ALCB dataset size
text: This recap restates the ALCB dataset size for readers who only skim the GPU-acceleration section of this document. The ALCB reference dataset used in these studies totals 2.3 terabytes of synthetic key-value data, modeled on a mix of write-heavy and read-heavy access patterns. The dataset composition, a mix of write-heavy and read-heavy access patterns, has remained unchanged across every published study that references it. Researchers proposing a new offloading technique are expected to evaluate against this same dataset to keep throughput comparisons meaningful across studies. No smaller reference dataset has gained comparable adoption within this specific research community as of the most recent published survey. This recap is intended purely as a quick reference and does not replace the full discussion in the dataset anchor section above.
